agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Order cancellation feature for an order management system. Three stakeholders (Product, Finance, Operations) have provided requirements that govern when an order may be cancelled relative to its lifecycle states (placed, invoiced, shipped).","bounded_context":"Order Management / Order Lifecycle","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Order","definition":"A commercial commitment by a customer to purchase goods, progressing through lifecycle states from placement to shipment."},{"term":"Cancellation","definition":"The act of voiding an order, reversing the commitment. Subject to business rules governing eligibility."},{"term":"Invoice","definition":"A financial document recording the obligation to pay. Once created, it is irreversible per finance policy."},{"term":"Placement","definition":"The initial act of creating an order. Per operations, this is the moment invoicing occurs."},{"term":"Shipment","definition":"The physical dispatch of goods to the customer. Per product, this is the cutoff after which cancellation is impossible."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"An order can be cancelled at any time before it ships.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a product policy defining the cancellation window. A VP could change this to allow post-ship cancellation (returns) or narrow the window. Source: Product Management.","testable_assertion":"Given an order in any pre-shipment state, when cancellation is requested, then the cancellation succeeds."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Orders that have been invoiced cannot be cancelled. Invoicing is irreversible.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Finance treats the invoice as an immutable financial record. Cancelling an invoiced order would corrupt the financial ledger. This is a data/state integrity constraint — reversing an invoice is not a policy choice but a ledger consistency requirement.","testable_assertion":"Given an order that has been invoiced, when cancellation is requested, then the cancellation is rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"Orders are invoiced at the time of placement to lock in pricing.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is an operational policy to freeze pricing. A VP could decide to defer invoicing to a later stage (e.g., at shipment) without corrupting system state. Source: Operations.","testable_assertion":"Given an order is placed, when placement completes, then an invoice record exists for that order."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Order placed and immediately cancelled","expected_behavior":"CONTRADICTED: BR-1 says this should succeed (pre-shipment). BR-2 + BR-3 say this must fail (already invoiced at placement). Cannot define expected behavior without resolution."},{"scenario":"Order placed but shipment is delayed for days","expected_behavior":"CONTRADICTED: Customer expects cancellation is possible (BR-1), but the order was invoiced at placement (BR-3), making it uncancellable (BR-2). The cancellation window is effectively zero."},{"scenario":"Partial shipment — some items shipped, some not","expected_behavior":"Ambiguous even without the contradiction. Would need guidance on whether cancellation applies at line-item or order level."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH — If BR-2+BR-3 prevail, customers can never cancel orders, leading to chargebacks, refund disputes, and potential regulatory exposure. If BR-1 prevails, finance must support invoice reversal or credit notes, impacting revenue recognition and audit trails.","operational":"Resolving this contradiction will fundamentally change the order lifecycle design. Deferring invoicing changes pricing lock-in strategy. Allowing invoice reversal changes the financial system's immutability model.","user":"Customers expect to cancel orders before shipment (industry standard). A system where cancellation is impossible after placement would be a severe UX failure and likely increase support volume dramatically.","risk":"Shipping this contradiction unresolved guarantees one of: (a) broken financial integrity, (b) zero cancellation capability, or (c) undefined system behavior at runtime. All are unacceptable.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Cannot cancel orders despite reasonable expectation they can. Leads to dissatisfaction, chargebacks, and churn."},{"group":"Finance Team","impact":"If forced to allow cancellation, must handle invoice reversal or credit notes — a fundamental change to their ledger model."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"If invoicing is deferred to enable cancellation, pricing lock-in strategy must be redesigned (e.g., price reservation without invoicing)."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"Will bear the brunt of customer complaints if cancellation is blocked, or finance exceptions if cancellation is forced through."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Consumer protection laws in many jurisdictions require a cancellation window. A system that invoices at placement and blocks cancellation may violate these regulations."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The three requirements contain a direct logical contradiction that makes it impossible to define correct behavior. BR-1 (cancel before shipment) is mutually exclusive with BR-2 + BR-3 (invoiced at placement, invoiced = uncancellable). No amount of analysis can resolve this without a stakeholder decision. Confidence is ≤ 50 per contradiction stop condition.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction is real and not a misreading — the three rules are logically incompatible","Invoice immutability (BR-2) is likely a true invariant rooted in financial integrity","The operational policy of invoicing at placement (BR-3) is the most likely rule to be relaxed"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which stakeholder's rule should yield — this is a business decision, not an analytical one","Whether 'invoiced' in the finance constraint means the same thing as 'invoiced' in the operations statement (possible language mismatch across bounded contexts)","Whether legal/regulatory requirements mandate a cancellation window, which would force a specific resolution"],"assumptions":["All three statements are authoritative and accurately represent each team's requirements","'Invoice' means the same thing to both Finance and Operations","There is no existing credit note or invoice reversal mechanism in the system"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"BR-1 (cancel before shipment) is logically incompatible with BR-2 + BR-3 combined. If orders are invoiced at placement (BR-3) and invoiced orders cannot be cancelled (BR-2), then orders can never be cancelled — rendering BR-1 impossible. The cancellation window is mathematically zero.","affected_stakeholders":["Product Management","Finance Team","Operations Team","Customers","Legal/Compliance"],"options":["(A) Defer invoicing to shipment time — preserves BR-1 and BR-2, changes BR-3. Operations must find another mechanism to lock pricing (e.g., price reservation or quote snapshot).","(B) Introduce credit notes — preserve BR-3, relax BR-2 to allow financial reversal via credit notes rather than invoice deletion. Cancellation triggers a credit note, not an invoice deletion, preserving the audit trail.","(C) Distinguish 'pro-forma invoice' from 'final invoice' — Operations issues a pro-forma at placement (locks pricing, but is reversible), Finance's irreversibility rule applies only to final invoices issued at shipment.","(D) Narrow the cancellation window — reject BR-1 as stated, define a short grace period before invoicing completes (e.g., 15 minutes). Requires async invoicing."],"recommendation":"Option (C) is the most likely intended design. It satisfies all three teams: Operations locks pricing at placement via pro-forma, Finance maintains invoice immutability for final invoices, and Product gets the pre-shipment cancellation window. However, this requires explicit stakeholder alignment — I cannot assume this is correct."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Contradictory requirements make it impossible to define a correct implementation","business_rule_broken":"BR-1 is mutually exclusive with the combination of BR-2 and BR-3. Any implementation will necessarily violate at least one stated requirement."}]}
